2603.17624.title: Do Language Models Encode Semantic Relations? Probing and Sparse Feature Analysis
2603.17624.paper_summary: Understanding whether large language models (LLMs) capture structured meaning requires examining how they represent concept relationships. In this work, we study three models of increasing scale: Pythia-70M, GPT-2, and Llama 3.1 8B, focusing on four semantic relations: synonymy, antonymy, hypernymy, and hyponymy. We combine linear probing with mechanistic interpretability techniques, including sparse autoencoders (SAE) and activation patching, to identify where these relations are encoded and how specific features contribute to their representation. Our results reveal a directional asymmetry in hierarchical relations: hypernymy is encoded redundantly and resists suppression, while hyponymy relies on compact features that are more easily disrupted by ablation. More broadly, relation signals are diffuse but exhibit stable profiles: they peak in the mid-layers and are stronger in post-residual/MLP pathways than in attention. Difficulty is consistent across models (antonymy easiest, synonymy hardest). Probe-level causality is capacity-dependent: on Llama 3.1, SAE-guided patching reliably shifts these signals, whereas on smaller models the shifts are weak or unstable. Our results clarify where and how reliably semantic relations are represented inside LLMs, and provide a reproducible framework for relating sparse features to probe-level causal evidence.
2603.17624.paper_summary_zh: 理解大型語言模型（LLMs）是否捕捉結構化意義需要檢視它們如何表示概念之間的關係。<lb/>在這項研究中，我們研究了三個逐漸擴大的模型：Pythia-70M、GPT-2 和 Llama 3.1 8B，重點關注四種語義關係：同義、反義、上位詞和下位詞。<lb/>我們結合線性探測與機械解釋技術，包括稀疏自編碼器（SAE）和激活修補，來識別這些關係的編碼位置以及特定特徵如何貢獻於它們的表示。<lb/>我們的結果揭示了層級關係中的方向性不對稱：上位詞以冗餘的方式編碼並抵抗抑制，而下位詞則依賴於更容易受到消融影響的緊湊特徵。<lb/>更廣泛地說，關係信號是分散的，但展現出穩定的輪廓：它們在中層達到峰值，並且在後殘差/MLP 路徑中的強度大於在注意力中的強度。<lb/>各模型之間的難度是一致的（反義最容易，同義最難）。<lb/>探測級因果關係依賴於容量：在 Llama 3.1 上，SAE 引導的修補可靠地改變這些信號，而在較小的模型上，這些變化則較弱或不穩定。<lb/>我們的結果澄清了語義關係在 LLMs 內部的表示位置和可靠性，並提供了一個可重複的框架，將稀疏特徵與探測級因果證據聯繫起來。
2603.17624.author: Andor Diera et.al.
